skill.name: architecture-decision-records
skill.description: Create, review, and maintain Architecture Decision Records that document technical decisions, alternatives, trade-offs, and long-term consequences.
skill.summary: Guides teams through practical Architecture Decision Record creation and lifecycle management.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution and ADR terminology as weak cryptography, but the reviewed file contains documentation templates rather than executable code. The only real concerns are benign ADR tooling command examples and external reference links, so publication is acceptable with normal command-review expectations.

low_findings.title: Benign ADR Tooling Command Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes example shell commands for adr-tools, including installation, ADR creation, table-of-contents generation, and linking. These are legitimate documentation workflow examples, but users should review them before execution because they can install software and modify documentation files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced section is clearly labeled as adr-tools automation and contains concrete shell commands. The commands match the skill purpose and show no malicious intent or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to PostgreSQL, Martin Fowler, EventStoreDB, MADR, ADR, and adr-tools resources. These are normal references for an ADR documentation skill and do not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are static reference links in Markdown. I found no evidence of network requests, credential handling, tracking parameters, or dynamic exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across Markdown fences and inline examples. Manual review found documentation formatting and ADR examples, not executable Ruby code paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown code fences, inline code, or example documentation content. No interpreter invocation or user-controlled command execution path is present in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators in ADR prose and examples. Manual review found architecture documentation text and filenames, not cryptographic APIs, hashing routines, or security-sensitive implementations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain ADR descriptions, table entries, database discussion, or directory examples. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithm usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples in Markdown
dangerous_patterns.description: A fenced bash block shows adr-tools commands. This is not malicious, but agents should not execute installation or file-writing commands without clear user intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples for ADR workflows. They can affect the local environment if executed, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: Important technical decisions often lose context after implementation. This skill helps teams write ADRs that preserve rationale, alternatives, consequences, and lifecycle status.
actual_capabilities: Provides standard, lightweight, Y-statement, deprecation, and RFC-style ADR templates.
Explains when to write an ADR and when a small implementation note is enough.
Guides teams through context, decision drivers, options, rationale, and consequences.
Shows how to organize ADR files, indexes, statuses, and related decision links.
Includes review checklist guidance for submission, review, acceptance, and follow-up.
Gives examples for database, frontend, API gateway, migration, and event sourcing decisions.
limitations: It does not verify whether an architecture decision is technically correct.
It does not replace review by senior engineers or affected teams.
It does not automatically create repository files unless the user asks the agent to edit files.
It does not provide legal, compliance, or security approval for a chosen architecture.

use_cases.title: Record a Major Technology Choice
use_cases.description: Document why a team selected a database, framework, API gateway, or infrastructure pattern.

use_cases.title: Preserve Context for Future Maintainers
use_cases.description: Capture constraints, alternatives, rationale, and trade-offs before implementation details are forgotten.

use_cases.title: Standardize Decision Review
use_cases.description: Create a consistent ADR process with statuses, indexes, related decisions, and review checklists.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic ADR
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a first Architecture Decision Record.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me draft an ADR for choosing [technology or approach]. Include context, decision drivers, considered options, decision, rationale, and consequences.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request that needs structured trade-off analysis.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an ADR section comparing [option A], [option B], and [option C] for [system context]. Include pros, cons, risks, and decision drivers.

prompt_templates.title: Update an ADR Lifecycle
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a superseded or deprecated decision.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write an ADR that supersedes ADR-[number]. Explain what changed, why the old decision no longer fits, and the migration consequences.

prompt_templates.title: Design an ADR Process
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an ADR workflow for my engineering organization. Include directory structure, status rules, review checklist, index format, and maintenance practices.

output_examples.input: We need to decide between PostgreSQL and MongoDB for a new order service.
output: A structured ADR with context, decision drivers, options, rationale, and consequences.
A balanced comparison of consistency, query needs, team experience, and operational cost.
A final decision section that explains why the selected database fits the service.

output_examples.input: Our old ADR selected MongoDB, but we are migrating to PostgreSQL.
output: A deprecation ADR that references the earlier decision and explains the changed conditions.
A migration plan with phases, risks, positive consequences, and negative consequences.
A lifecycle update that marks the old ADR as superseded or deprecated.

output_examples.input: We want every team to document architecture decisions consistently.
output: A recommended ADR directory structure and README index format.
A review checklist for submission, review, and post-acceptance tasks.
Status definitions for proposed, accepted, rejected, deprecated, and superseded records.

best_practices: Write ADRs before implementation starts, while options and constraints are still clear.
Document real trade-offs, including negative consequences and rejected options.
Create new ADRs to supersede accepted decisions instead of rewriting history.
anti_patterns: Using an ADR for minor patches, routine maintenance, or small implementation details.
Recording only the final decision without context, alternatives, or consequences.
Leaving accepted ADRs without status updates when later decisions replace them.

faq.question: What is an Architecture Decision Record?
faq.answer: An ADR is a short document that captures an important technical decision, its context, alternatives, rationale, and consequences.

faq.question: When should I write an ADR?
faq.answer: Write one for significant architecture, technology, integration, security, or process decisions that future maintainers should understand.

faq.question: Can this skill choose the best architecture for me?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure the decision process, but your team must validate the technical and business fit.

faq.question: Which ADR formats does it support?
faq.answer: It includes standard MADR-style records, lightweight records, Y-statements, deprecation records, and RFC-style proposals.

faq.question: Does it manage ADR files automatically?
faq.answer: It explains file organization and adr-tools commands, but file changes require explicit user direction in the workspace.

faq.question: How should teams maintain old ADRs?
faq.answer: Keep accepted ADRs unchanged, then add new ADRs that deprecate or supersede decisions when circumstances change.
